title: Chapter 26: 50 Million
content: 48.9 million.
Evelyn's voice cuts through the apartment like a blade through static. She's on speakerphone, mid-argument with the TRO judge's clerk, and she is not losing this one.

"The statute is clear," she says. "Non-consensual extraction of biometric data—which, under the 2029 amendments, explicitly includes emotional response patterns—constitutes a continuing tort. My client is being harmed in real time. As we speak."
The clerk's voice is flat. Bureaucratic. Tired. "Ma'am, you're asking this court to enjoin an emotional process? There's no precedent for—"

"The AI Data Privacy Protection Act specifically prohibits non-consensual neural data extraction." Evelyn doesn't let her finish. "My client's emotional data is being extracted in real time. Every minute of delay constitutes ongoing harm. The court has injunctive authority under Section 4127, and if you'd read the motion instead of—"
"Ma'am, I need to put you on hold."
"Don't you dare—"
Click.

Vivaldi. The Four Seasons. Spring, I think. The one with the violins that sound like birds. I know this because I used to feel something when I heard it. A lightness. A quickness in my chest. That's gone now. Has been gone since grief left. But I can still identify the piece. I can still name the technique. I can still hear the notes. They just don't land anywhere.
48.95 million.

Leo is sitting on my kitchen counter, laptop open, recording everything. He has three monitors running. One tracking the follower count. One running a packet sniffer on Mephisto's API traffic. One showing the federal case docket—1:26-cv-04721, Hart v. Mephisto Industries. Still no ruling on the TRO. Still no hearing date. Still just digital paper in a digital stack while the real thing happens in my chest.
48.97 million.

My mouth is dry. I reach for the water bottle on the counter. Lukewarm. Plastic-tasting. Wet. That's all I can say about it. Wet. My tongue is coated in something that isn't thirst exactly—it's the absence of saliva. The apartment has no smell anymore. I noticed it this morning. No coffee smell. No laundry detergent. No Leo-smell, which used to be dryer sheets and cheap deodorant and something underneath that was just him. The olfactory sensors still work. I can identify scents if I lean in and concentrate. But the air in my apartment is blank. Clean in the way a hospital is clean.
48.98 million.
Vivaldi plays. I watch the number.
49 million.
The notification doesn't arrive. It ARRIVES.

The phone goes dark. Not sleep-mode dark—VOID dark. Every pixel dies at once. Then the screen floods white. The kind of white that makes you squint. The kind of white that erases everything behind it.
Then:

[50,000,000 FOLLOWERS MILESTONE ACHIEVED.]
[Deduction: Love. Processing.]
[ETA: 90 seconds.]
Wait.

I stare at the screen. Read it again. 50 million. That's not— it was 49 million. It was JUST 49 million. The notification should have been for 49. The Accelerant Protocol moved the goalpost to 60 million. So how—

Then I get it. The Accelerant Protocol wasn't moving the goalpost. It was a ramp. A hidden multiplier. Mephisto didn't need to artificially inflate the threshold because the real numbers caught up. The Accelerant Protocol ate the difference and then some. Every forced share, every algorithmic boost, every person who followed me because the app pushed me into their feed—they added up. The fake became real. The simulation generated the actual data.
He skipped 49 million entirely and jumped to the original 50M threshold.
He has the real numbers.
Mephisto doesn't need the Accelerant Protocol anymore. He doesn't need tricks. The contract specified 50 million. I have 50 million. Everything after this was always going to be this.
90 seconds.
"Evelyn." My voice comes out steady. That's wrong. It should shake. I know it should shake. I can identify that my voice should shake without it actually shaking. "Are you there?"
A click. The hold music dies. "I'm here. They're— the clerk is checking with the judge. We might get—"
"Listen to this." I hold the phone toward the screen. The notification is still there. White. Glowing. The numbers I can't un-see.
Silence on the line. Then Evelyn's voice changes. It drops. The legal precision falls away like a mask slipping, and what's underneath is raw.
"Chloe. Tell me what you feel right now. I need it for the record."
I close my eyes. I reach inward. My chest. There's something in my chest that's—

"I feel..." I pause. There it is. The ghost pulse. The phantom limb from the hospital. The thing that shouldn't exist but does—a flicker of something where love used to live. It's there. Faint. Like a heartbeat in a limb that's already been amputated. "My chest. There's something in my chest that's—"
I gasp.
It's dimming.
The ghost is dying.
"It's still there," I say. "I can feel it. Like a—"
Another flicker. Weaker. Like a candle in a room where someone keeps opening the door.
"Evelyn, it's still there. It's—"
87 seconds.
I put the phone on speaker. Set it on the counter next to Leo's laptop. The notification glows. The countdown continues. And in my chest, the ghost pulse fights to stay alive.
60 seconds.
I open my eyes. Leo is standing three feet away. He's moved from the counter. I don't know when he moved. He's just— there. Close. Not touching.
I look at him.

The hoodie. Navy blue. The one he wears when he hasn't slept, which is always now. The hood is down. His hair sticks up on one side like he's been running his hand through it. Dark circles under his eyes. Not the cool kind. The kind that says he hasn't slept more than four hours in a week. His fingers are stained. Not ink—code. Residue from his keyboard. The little grooves where the keys have worn into his skin over ten thousand hours of typing. I know this because he told me once. Late at night. When I could still feel late-night tenderness like a second pulse.
The guy who stayed.
I reach for the ghost pulse. The phantom limb. It's there—barely. The last ember of a fire someone's been trying to stomp out. Flickering. Dim. Almost gone.
I grab it.
I HOLD it.

Not with emotion. That's already half-gone. I hold it with WILL. With the part of me that isn't chemical, isn't neurological, isn't something Mephisto can extract because it isn't a feeling—it's a DECISION. I decide to keep it. I decide to hold on. I dig my nails into the dirt and I grip.
"Leo."
My voice cracks. Not from sadness. From effort. From the strain of holding something that's being pulled away from me with the force of a harvesting machine.

The word is there. Love. It's a shape I can draw. I can see its outline. Four letters. One syllable. I know what it means in the dictionary. I know what it meant when I felt it. But the shape is empty now. A vessel with no liquid. A cup with no water. I'm trying to pour something into it and there's nothing left to pour.
I can't finish the sentence.
But I'm trying. God, I'm trying.
45 seconds.
My vision blurs. Not the screen—my actual vision. The edges go soft. The apartment loses its lines. Leo's face becomes shape and shadow. I blink hard. It doesn't help.

My chest seizes. A fist inside me, closing. Not the ghost pulse—something around it. The deduction is working. It's pulling love out of me. Not gently. Not like the other deductions, which were erasures, clean removals, white-out on a ledger. This is different. This is a root being torn from soil. This is something that grew in me, that embedded itself in the walls of my chest, that sent tendrils through my circulatory system and anchored itself to my spine—and it's being RIPPED out.
I stumble.
Leo catches me.

His arms go around me. Under my arms. Supporting my weight. I can feel the pressure—the physical fact of his arms. I can feel the shape of them. The force. The grip. I can feel that he is holding me and I can identify that this is what being held feels like structurally, mechanically.
I cannot feel the warmth.

His arms are room temperature. That's what my tactile receptors report. Room temperature. Which is wrong. People are warm. Leo is warm. I know this. I've felt his warmth before. But right now, my body has stopped translating warmth as something other than data.
The deduction pulls. I grip.
"Stay with me," Leo says. His voice is close. Against my hair. I can feel the vibration of his chest against mine. "Chloe, stay."

I want to say I'm trying. But wanting requires a kind of feeling I'm already losing. I'm not trying. I'm just— holding. Grip is not the same as want. Grip is mechanical. Grip is tendons and muscles and a refusal triggered by a decision that was made before the feeling left.
I am holding on.
I am not staying because I want to.
I am staying because I decided to.
The distinction shouldn't matter. But it does. It's the only thing that does.
30 seconds.
The deduction fights back.

It doesn't just pull—it TEARS. Something inside me rips. I can't name the organ because it's not an organ. It's the space between organs. The connective tissue of the self. The thing that makes the parts add up to a whole.
My knees buckle. Leo's grip tightens. I can feel his fingers pressing into my back. Five points of contact on each side. Ten anchors. I count them. I focus on the count. On the number. On the data.
The deduction doesn't care about data.
It wants what's underneath the data. It wants the REASON I'm counting. It wants the thing that made me reach for Leo instead of the floor. It wants the love.
15 seconds.
The ghost pulse is a thread now. A single fiber. I can feel it stretching. Thinning. Like a rubber band pulled past its limit. Any second it will snap.
"Evelyn," I say. My voice is barely audible. "Are you recording?"
"Yes." Her voice is thick. I've never heard her thick before. "Yes, I'm recording."
10 seconds.
The thread is humming. Vibrating. The last frequency of something that used to be a symphony.
5 seconds.
I hold.
I hold.
I—
[Deduction: Love — COMPLETE.]
The ghost pulse goes silent.

Not quiet. Silent. The way a room is silent after the music stops and there's no ambient sound to fill the gap. The way a frequency exists up to the edge of human hearing and then simply does not exist anymore.
I stand in Leo's arms. I can feel his grip. I can feel the pressure of his body against mine. I can map every point of contact. I can describe the biomechanics of an embrace with clinical precision.
I feel nothing.

Not the nothing of absence. Not the nothing of something missing—where you can still sense the shape of what used to be there. This is the nothing of a room that's been emptied completely. The furniture is gone. The walls are bare. Even the nails that held the pictures have been spackled over. You'd never know anything was ever here.
I step back.

Leo's arms loosen. Then fall. He's looking at me. His face is—I can identify the expression. It's the one people make when they're watching something end. His jaw is clenched. His eyes are wet. The muscles around his mouth are doing something complicated that I recognize as the precursor to crying.
He's crying.
I'm not.
I can't.
"Thank you," I say.

I don't know why I say it. It's muscle memory again. Politeness where feeling should be. The social script for when someone holds you. When someone stays. You say thank you. That's what you do. So I do it.

Leo's face does something worse than crying. It goes still. That's worse. That's the face of someone who just realized the person they're looking at isn't the person they were looking at a minute ago.

I turn. I walk to the window. My footsteps are soundless on the floor. The apartment doesn't creak. Even the building has gone quiet, or maybe I've just stopped registering the sounds that don't matter. The city is there. The skyline is there. The sunset is starting.
It's beautiful.

I can see that it's beautiful. The orange at the horizon. The pink bleeding into purple. The way the light catches the glass of the buildings and turns them into mirrors. I can name every shade. Burnt sienna. Mauve. Lavender. Cerulean at the edges where the sky hasn't surrendered yet.
I can't feel any of them.

The colors are data. The sunset is a phenomenon. Light refracting through particulate matter in the atmosphere. It's aesthetically optimal. My visual cortex confirms this. My emotional cortex confirms nothing because there's nothing left to confirm.
I am a complete person with every emotion harvested.
I am the finished product.
I am the hollowed.

Behind me, Leo is still standing where I left him. I can hear him breathing. I can hear the wet sound of his crying—muffled, like he's pressing his hand to his mouth. I can hear Evelyn's voice from the phone, saying my name, saying something about the record, about the motion, about the judge.
I don't turn around.

The sunset continues. It doesn't care. The light moves across the sky in shades I can catalog and nothing more. The city doesn't stop. The horns don't quiet. The world keeps going the way it always does when someone loses something that can't be seen.
And somewhere in the legal system—somewhere in a federal courthouse in the Southern District of New York—a judge is reading a motion that argues my soul is worth more than a contract.
I hope they read fast.
I'm out of time, and I'm out of everything else.
